APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — the paper addresses AMR parsing and realization using neural seq2seq models with large-scale self-training on Gigaword, a topic squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues. It targets the right audience by comparing to standard AMR baselines (JAMR, CAMR, PBMT) and reports accepted metrics (SMATCH, BLEU) with competitive results (61.9 SMATCH, 32.3 BLEU). The problem formulation (paired parser/realizer), methods (stacked BiLSTM seq2seq with attention, linearization, anonymization, data augmentation via back-translation-style self-training) and evaluations are exactly those expected by the community. The paper is well positioned within prior work and uses standard evaluation protocols and baselines, making it a strong fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of data-sparse AMR parsing/realization and demonstrates that seq2seq models can be made effective by leveraging millions of unlabeled Gigaword sentences via a paired self-training procedure (Algorithm 1) and careful preprocessing. Empirically it achieves competitive 61.9 SMATCH for parsing without heavy external semantic resources and a +5 point improvement to 32.3 BLEU for realization, challenging the belief that seq2seq is unsuitable for AMR and showing robustness to graph linearization choices. These results are likely to change practitioners' approaches (encouraging large-scale unlabeled pretraining and anonymization strategies), open follow-up work on pretraining/self-training for structured semantic tasks, and be adopted beyond AMR, but stop short of a field‑transforming breakthrough.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has an overall logical structure (Related Work → Tasks → Model → Data Handling → Experiments/Results) and clearly states the main goals and training procedure (e.g., Equations 1–2 and Algorithm 1). However, presentation quality is degraded by numerous duplicated and garbled passages (e.g., the repeated “While AMR is extremely expressive…” and repeated Alignment/Grammar paragraphs), stray TeX/footnote artifacts, and missing or unrendered figures/tables referenced in the text, which make some sections hard to follow. Notation for the main tasks is defined and usable, but inconsistent formatting and editorial errors reduce clarity and would benefit from careful cleanup and consolidation of repeated content.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies sequence-to-sequence models to both AMR parsing and realization and introduces a paired self-training / pretraining procedure that bootstraps a parser on millions of unlabeled Gigaword sentences to overcome data sparsity—an approach explicitly contrasted with prior alignment-, grammar- and SMT-based systems (sections on Data Augmentation and Related Work). It also experimentally shows seq2seq models are largely agnostic to graph linearization order and minimizes use of alignments, which is a non-trivial and clearly differentiated extension of existing methods (e.g., Peng & Xue 2017, JAMR, CAMR). The problem (AMR parsing/realization) is well-studied, so the contribution is notable rather than wholly novel, and would be informative to experts.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear main claims (e.g., achieving 61.9 SMATCH for parsing and 32.3 BLEU for realization, robustness to linearization) and outlines the paired self-training procedure (Alg.1) and seq2seq model, but the provided text lacks full experimental detail (complete tables, dataset splits, hyper-parameters, and statistical significance testing) needed to fully validate those claims. The mathematical/formal parts are minimal (simple argmax objectives and seq2seq description) and are correct. The methodology is reasonable in broad strokes and compares to prior systems, but there are missing controls and details (e.g., how unlabeled data is sampled/filtered, exact baselines used) and a minor inconsistency: the paper claims minimal use of alignments yet explicitly uses JAMR/unsupervised alignments for anonymization. Overall the conclusions appear plausible given reported results but are not completely substantiated by the evidence shown in the excerpt.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper reports quantitative results for both parsing (61.9 SMATCH) and realization (32.3 BLEU), describes a concrete paired-training procedure (Algorithm 1), and evaluates linearization strategies (Section 7) showing the model is largely agnostic to graph-to-sequence order — indicating substantial experimental coverage and some sensitivity analysis. It also situates the work in a thorough related-work review (alignment-, grammar-, neural-based systems and prior realization methods). However, discussion of failure modes and limitations is sparse (only brief notes about information loss from variable removal), and while preprocessing, anonymization and the high-level model architecture are described, many low-level reproduction details (exact hyperparameters, training schedules, full ablation tables in the provided excerpt) are missing.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to a wide set of appropriate and recent baselines (JAMR/Flanigan 2014, CAMR/Wang 2016, Pust et al. 2015, Pourdamghani et al. 2016, Peng & Xue 2017, Barzdins & Gosko 2016, Song et al. 2016, etc.), reports standard metrics (SMATCH for parsing, BLEU for realization), and presents a results table with those systems. It explicitly discusses methodological differences (e.g., use of unlabeled Gigaword pretraining vs. external semantic resources, anonymization strategies, linearization choices) and acknowledges when other methods obtain higher scores while clarifying differing resource conditions (‘‘current best reported without significant use of external annotated semantic resources’’). Overall the comparisons are comprehensive and the paper analyzes why performance differs from key competitors.
